nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; commonly found along coral reefs, sea walls, pilings, and harbors, typically hiding in crevices and caves during daylight hours.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet of meaty foods including mysid shrimp, finely chopped marine flesh, frozen crustacean flesh, bloodworms, and vitamin-enriched prepared foods. Feeding after lights-out tends to improve acceptance, as this species is nocturnal in its feeding habits.

summary: A striking, fiery-red cardinalfish from the Western Atlantic, generally considered beginner-friendly and reef-safe with caution — it has been known to prey on ornamental shrimp. Best kept singly or as a mated pair; tends to be aggressive toward conspecifics.
careNotes:
The Flame Cardinalfish (also known as the Flamefish) is one of the most commonly encountered cardinalfishes in the aquarium trade. It is a strictly nocturnal species; in the wild it hides in crevices and caves during the day and emerges to feed at night. In aquariums it generally adapts to daytime feeding over time, though offering food after the lights dim often improves initial acceptance. Providing ample rockwork with caves and overhangs is generally advised — the species appears to settle more readily and exhibit calmer behaviour when it has retreat options.

Water parameters should be kept stable in the typical marine reef range: temperature roughly 22–27°C, pH 8.1–8.4, specific gravity 1.020–1.025, and alkalinity around 8–12 dKH.

Toward conspecifics, this species tends to be highly aggressive in smaller aquariums. Sources consistently advise keeping it singly or as an established mated pair; attempts to keep groups have been known to result in sustained fighting. The species is generally peaceful toward dissimilar fish.

Reef safety is conditional: the fish itself has not been reported to nip corals, but it has been known to consume ornamental shrimp and small crustaceans, so reef tanks with prized cleaner or decorative shrimp may not be suitable without adequate rockwork to give invertebrates refuge.

Adult size has been reported up to approximately 11 cm (FishBase total length); most captive specimens appear to reach 10–11 cm. Lifespan data in captivity are not widely documented in available sources.

Temperament disagreement across sources: LiveAquaria categorized this species as 'aggressive', while BlueZoo Aquatics and AquariumDomain both listed it as 'semi-aggressive'. The semi-aggressive designation has been used here as it better reflects the nuance — aggression is primarily directed at conspecifics and small crustacean prey, not toward dissimilar tankmates.
breedingNotes: The Flame Cardinalfish has been known to spawn in captivity. It is a paternal mouthbrooder: after spawning, the male carries the egg mass in his mouth for several weeks and stops eating during this period. Visual signs of brooding include a visibly swollen jaw and reduced interest in food. Survival rates for fry in community systems tend to be low due to predation pressure; dedicated breeding setups generally improve outcomes. Spawning has been documented year-round in wild populations.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Apogon maculatus confirmed), adult size (4.5 in / 11.4 cm), tank size, care level, temperament, and breeding behaviour. Designated secondary source in manifest; note the source name in instructions said 'FishBase' but the URL resolves to BlueZoo Aquatics.

additional.name: FishBase (fishbase.se mirror)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Apogonidae, order Kurtiformes), confirmed max size 11.1 cm TL, temperature 22.9–28°C, native range (Western Atlantic from Canada to Brazil), and nocturnal mouthbrooder behaviour. FishBase.org returned HTTP 403; fishbase.se mirror used instead.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4 in / 10.2 cm), temperature (74–82°F / 23.3–27.8°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), tank size (30 gal), care level (easy), temperament (semi-aggressive), diet, reef-safe status with caution re ornamental shrimp, and conspecific aggression.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; has spawned in captivity. Males carry egg mass for several weeks without eating. Fry survival in community tanks tends to be low.
